embeds.title: ⭐ Creating a Minecraft Server
embeds.description: Three ways to host a server — pick the one that fits your budget and skill level. All methods below work on current **and** older versions.

fields.name: 🟢 Option 1 — Free Online Host  ·  Easiest
fields.value:
**Cost:** Free  ·  **Difficulty:** Easy

Make a free account on [**Aternos**](https://aternos.org), create a server, choose your version, and hit **Start**. Share the address with friends — no setup on your end.
-# Free servers sleep when empty and have limited RAM. For 24/7 uptime and more power, paid hosts like Shockbyte, BisectHosting or Apex are worth it.

fields.name: 🔵 Option 2 — Host with Essential  ·  Moderate
fields.value:
**Cost:** Free  ·  **Difficulty:** Moderate

Install the [**Essential Mod**](https://essential.gg/download), open your world in-game, and click **Invite Friends** — Bedrock-style P2P, no port forwarding.
⚠️ -# Essential is phasing out World Hosting in an upcoming update. It still works on current & older versions for now.

fields.name: 🔴 Option 3 — Self-Host on Your PC  ·  Advanced
fields.value:
**Cost:** Free  ·  **Difficulty:** Hard

1. Install [**Java**](https://www.java.com/download) and grab the official [**server.jar**](https://www.minecraft.net/en-us/download/server).
2. Put it in its own folder and launch it with the start script below.
3. Accept the EULA (`eula.txt`), then port-forward **25565** — or skip that with [**playit.gg**](https://playit.gg) for a free public address.

fields.name: 📄 Sample  start.bat
fields.value:
```bat
@echo off
java -Xmx2G -Xms2G -jar server.jar nogui
pause
```
-# `-Xmx2G` = max RAM. Bump to 4G/6G for more players.
